Here is a real example. The Basware API (public API behaviour, nothing customer-specific) returns the invoice attachment as a redirect:

GET /v1/accountingDocuments/{id}/attachments/{attachmentId}
-> 302, Location: https://api.us.basware.com/cfsecure/.../INVOICE%20%28copy%202%29%202026.pdf
   ?Expires=...&Signature=W-4m2LqUesX~Bxpzq6CfZ~Upf87...&Key-Pair-Id=...

The second call to that pre-signed URL fails with 403 Forbidden. But the same URL works in Postman or Bruno with “follow redirects” on. Why?

Turn on the trace and compare the Location you received with the URL CPI really sent (RealDestinationUrl). They are different:

Path:    received  ...INVOICE%20%28copy%202%29%202026.pdf
         CPI sent   ...INVOICE%20(copy%202)%202026.pdf      (%28/%29 decoded to "(" ")")
Query:   received  ...W-4m2LqUesX~Bxpzq6CfZ~Upf87...
         CPI sent   ...W-4m2LqUesX%7EBxpzq6CfZ%7EUpf87...   (every "~" encoded to %7E)

A pre-signed URL is signed over the exact bytes of the URL. If one character changes its encoding, the signature no longer matches, and you get a 403. Postman sends the URL exactly as it is. CPI changes it.

Note: this is not an AWS problem. It happens with any URL where the exact encoding must stay the same — AWS S3/CloudFront, Azure SAS tokens, Google Cloud signed URLs, and others.

Why CPI changes the URL

The answer is not in the SAP Help Portal — it is in the Apache Camel source code, which the HTTP adapter is built on. Two layers change the URL:

  • Camel UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder. Camel keeps a list ("BitSet") of characters it treats as unsafe and encodes them. The tilde is on that list (unsafeCharactersHttp.set('~') in the source), so every ~ becomes %7E.
  • Apache HttpClient. It normalizes the URI following RFC 3986. Parentheses are allowed in a path, so HttpClient decodes %28/%29 back to ( ).

Neither layer can be turned off in the adapter. If you want to check it yourself:

  • UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/core/camel-util/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/util/UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder.java
  • HttpProducer: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/components/camel-http/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/component/http/HttpProducer.java

Reading the Camel source is a good habit — it answers questions the docs leave open.

What does not fix it

  • CamelHttpUri / CamelHttpQuery headers. They only change which URL goes into the adapter. The adapter still runs the same encoder. (I confirmed this with a trace: still 403.)
  • Adapter settings. There is no “raw URL” or “disable encoding” option.
  • The %25 escaping trick (SAP KBA 3131448, which I also described in an earlier post, "SAP Cloud Integration — URL escaping hack"). It helps with characters the adapter decodes (the parentheses): send %2528, and it stays %28. But it cannot help with the tilde, because the adapter always encodes ~ to %7E and there is no input that keeps a plain ~. So a configuration-only fix is not possible here.

The fix: call the URL from Groovy

Since no setting stops the encoding, make the second call outside the adapter — from a Groovy script with java.net.URL. new URL(str) sends the URL exactly as it is, like a browser or Postman.

Replace the second HTTP call (the Request-Reply step and its receiver channel) with one Groovy script step that reads the pre-signed URL from the property and downloads the file:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.net.HttpURLConnection
import java.net.URL

/**
 * Downloads a file from a pre-signed URL captured from the 302 "Location" header
 * (stored in property TargetLocation).
 *
 * Why Groovy and not the HTTP adapter:
 * The CPI HTTP adapter (Apache Camel + HttpClient) always re-encodes the URL - it
 * turns '~' into '%7E' and decodes '%28'/'%29' to '(' ')'. A pre-signed URL is
 * signed over the exact bytes, so any change -> 403. java.net.URL sends the URL
 * exactly as it is.
 */
def Message processData(Message message) {

    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    def rawUrl = message.getProperty("TargetLocation")?.toString()

    // Guard: nothing to download
    if (rawUrl == null || rawUrl.trim().isEmpty()) {
        if (messageLog != null) {
            messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Termination_Reason", "Missing_TargetLocation")
        }
        throw new IllegalStateException("TargetLocation is empty - no URL to download")
    }

    HttpURLConnection conn = null
    try {
        URL url = new URL(rawUrl)                    // keeps the URL byte-for-byte
        conn = (HttpURLConnection) url.openConnection()
        conn.setRequestMethod("GET")
        conn.setInstanceFollowRedirects(true)
        conn.setConnectTimeout(60000)
        conn.setReadTimeout(60000)
        // Pre-signed URL is self-authenticating -> no Authorization header.

        int rc = conn.getResponseCode()
        if (messageLog != null) {
            messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Attachment_HTTP_Status", String.valueOf(rc))
        }

        if (rc >= 200 && rc < 300) {
            // Read the body in 8 KB chunks. Fine for normal documents;
            // for very large files, use a streaming pattern to avoid OOM.
            def buffer = new ByteArrayOutputStream()
            conn.getInputStream().withStream { input ->
                byte[] chunk = new byte[8192]
                int n
                while ((n = input.read(chunk)) != -1) {
                    buffer.write(chunk, 0, n)
                }
            }
            byte[] data = buffer.toByteArray()

            message.setBody(data)
            def ct = conn.getContentType()
            if (ct != null) { message.setHeader("Content-Type", ct) }
            message.setHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", rc)

            if (messageLog != null) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Attachment_Size_Bytes", String.valueOf(data.length))
            }
        } else {
            def err = ""
            try {
                def es = conn.getErrorStream()
                if (es != null) { es.withStream { err = it.getText("UTF-8") } }
            } catch (ignored) { }
            if (messageLog != null) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Termination_Reason", "Download_Failed")
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Error_Detail", "HTTP " + rc + ": " + err)
            }
            throw new RuntimeException("Download failed, HTTP " + rc + ": " + err)
        }
    } finally {
        if (conn != null) { conn.disconnect() }
    }

    return message
}

A note on using Groovy for HTTP

SAP recommends the HTTP adapter by default, and that is a good rule. But for a public pre-signed URL, the usual reasons against Groovy do not apply: no Cloud Connector, no client certificate, no proxy needed on Cloud Foundry. Two things still matter, and the script handles both: add MPL log entries so the call is visible in monitoring, and be careful with memory for very large files (stream instead of loading the full body).

Summary

  • The CPI HTTP adapter does not follow redirects — read the Location header and call again.
  • A 403 on a pre-signed URL is usually URL re-encoding, not authentication, and it is not AWS-specific.
  • The adapter re-encodes the URL in two layers (~ → %7E and %28 → (), and this cannot be turned off. When unsure, read the Camel source.
  • The %25 trick (KBA 3131448) fixes the parentheses but not the tilde.
  • To send the URL exactly as it is, use a Groovy script with java.net, and add MPL logs.

References

  • SAP KBA 3131448 — HTTP Receiver adapter discards encoding of special characters in address
  • SAP Cloud Integration — URL escaping hack (my earlier post): https://medium.com/@vbalko/sap-cloud-integration-url-escaping-hack-34a45f5a1f37
  • Apache Camel UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/core/camel-util/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/util/UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder.java
  • Apache Camel HttpProducer: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/components/camel-http/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/component/http/HttpProducer.java

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite: Smarter, Faster, More Connected Integrations - Q2'2026 News
entry.content:
Welcome to the latest product highlights for SAP Integration Suite. In this update, we’re sharing newly released capabilities designed to help you accelerate integration development, simplify operations, and connect SAP and third-party landscapes with greater confidence. From AI-assisted API and error-resolution features to new adapters, Edge Integration Cell enhancements, event-driven monitoring, and expanded data center availability, updates in Q2 2026 help integration teams build faster, run more reliably, and deliver more value across the enterprise. So, let’s dig into it!

Integration for AI

The introduction of API-Centric Integration and MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite bring API management, integration mediation, and agent readiness into one harmonized integration layer that enables customers to build, govern, and scale the next generation of enterprise integration scenarios, including AI-powered use cases.

With API-centric integration, practitioners can design APIs as first-class integration artifacts that combine routing, transformation, enrichment, logging, security policies, rate limiting, and lifecycle governance in one consistent authoring and runtime experience, rather than treating API proxies and integration flows as separate implementation layers. Building on this foundation, the MCP Gateway aggregates SAP APIs, non-SAP APIs, integration flows, data sources, and external MCP servers as governed, discoverable MCP tools for AI agents, while enforcing enterprise controls such as authentication, authorization, rate limiting, payload protection, monitoring, and traceability.

Together, these capabilities help customers turn existing business processes and APIs into secure, reusable, AI-ready interfaces that can be consumed by both traditional applications and agentic AI scenarios without sacrificing governance, operational visibility, or control. Find resources about these exciting updates: One Blog, Two Strategic Topics: API-Centric Integration and MCP Gateway

AI for Integration

These new AI-powered capabilities in SAP Integration Suite help you work faster across design, documentation, testing, and operations:

AI-assisted message processing error resolution

Restore integrations faster and operate with greater confidence by using embedded AI to identify where an error occurred, explain its likely root cause, highlight the first failed step, and recommend a resolution directly within SAP Integration Suite monitoring. By making analysis easy to copy, save, and share, the feature improves collaboration between integration teams, supports faster pattern recognition across recurring issues, reduces mean time to recovery, and lowers the learning curve for new developers, ultimately helping customers keep business-critical processes running more reliably while using AI capabilities in a guided and economical way. Documentation: AI-assisted Error Resolution

 AI-assisted API specification and documentation generation

This feature helps you accelerate API onboarding by using generative AI to derive OpenAPI specifications and documentation from back-end service definitions. Instead of manually modelling endpoints, request and response structures, parameters, and descriptions, teams can generate a standards-aligned API contract and supporting documentation as a starting point, then review and refine it before publishing. This reduces repetitive specification work, improves consistency across APIs, and helps developers expose back-end services faster with documentation that is easier to understand, test, and adopt. Documentation: Generate OpenAPI Specification with AI Assistance.

SAP Business Accelerator Hub

The API mocking capability in SAP Business Accelerator Hub help you test and validate APIs earlier by generating mock responses directly from OpenAPI specifications, including support for multiple response types, status codes, and example payloads. Developers can use an AI-generated API sandbox as part of the API try-out experience to execute operations such as GET, PUT, POST, and DELETE against mock endpoints using sample or custom request data, without depending on a live back-end implementation. This accelerates API evaluation, improves contract-first development and consumer onboarding, and gives customers a faster, lower-friction way to explore, test, and adopt APIs before production connectivity is available. Documentation: Trying Out APIs in a AI-generated API Sandbox.

Edge Integration Cell: Next-generation hybrid integration runtime

These Edge Integration Cell updates make the hybrid runtime more configurable, observable, and deployment-ready for distributed integration scenarios:

▪️ Support for the partner directory: Edge Integration Cell now supports use of the partner directory, enabling integration flows running on the edge runtime to read partner-specific configuration data at runtime. This makes it possible to externalize and dynamically resolve partner-related parameters instead of hard-coding them into integration logic, which simplifies the operation of multi-partner scenarios and reduces the effort required to maintain partner-specific variants. The value for customers is greater flexibility and scalability when running hybrid integrations across distributed partner landscapes. 

▪️ Custom system monitoring solution: Customers can now connect an external Prometheus instance to Edge Integration Cell using SAP-documented exporter endpoints and exposed monitoring data. Practitioners can configure Prometheus to scrape SAP metrics and then build dashboards and alerts in their existing observability tooling, with deployment options depending on TLS encryption and built-in monitoring requirements. This gives operations teams more control over how edge runtimes are monitored, supports integration into established monitoring landscapes, and helps customers detect issues earlier without relying only on default monitoring options.

▪️ Runtime activation on SAP Cloud Infrastructure: Edge Integration Cell runtime activation for SAP Integration Suite on SAP Cloud Infrastructure is now available on demand for supported regions, enabling hybrid integration scenarios between SAP Cloud Infrastructure-hosted SAP Integration Suite tenants and Edge Integration Cell runtime installations. This allows customers to place integration processing closer to regional workloads, helping reduce latency and support data residency or compliance requirements through sovereignty-aligned deployments. For integration teams, the update expands deployment choice and makes edge-based runtime adoption more practical for customers operating in regulated or region-sensitive environments.

Event-Driven Architecture

Support for health monitoring of SAP landscapes and advanced event mesh brokers through SAP Cloud ALM enables practitioners to monitor broker health and key SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh metrics from a central SAP Cloud ALM dashboard. By exposing broker metrics directly in the monitoring entry point used for SAP landscapes, teams can more easily observe event-driven integration infrastructure, identify potential issues earlier, and align event mesh operations with existing SAP landscape monitoring processes. This gives customers better operational visibility, faster issue detection, and a more consistent way to manage the reliability of event-driven integration scenarios. Documentation: Access SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh Metrics on SAP Cloud ALM Dashboard

Connectivity & Adapters: pre-built content for faster time to value

The following adapters and integration content were released in Q2 2026 to help customers connect SAP and third-party applications faster with prebuilt, standardized connectivity:

  • Adobe Sign Adapter
  • Google Workspace Adapter 
  • JDBC Receiver Adapter
  • Twilio Adapter

New Data Center Availability

  • Europe (Frankfurt) through Microsoft Azure.
  • Japan (Tokyo) through Google Cloud.
  • United States (Sterling) on SAP-hosted infrastructure

More details on SAP Discovery Center Service - SAP Integration Suite.

--

➡️To see some of these updates in action, join our monthly update webinar on July 28th. Every last Tuesday of the month you have the chance to meet product experts and get a deep dive into the latest features for SAP Integration Suite - don't miss it!

entry.title: Migrating Interfaces with Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Migrating Interfaces with Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite 

This article explains how to migrate integration interfaces that rely on Dynamic Configuration (Adapter-Specific Message Attributes, ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration (PO) to SAP Cloud Integration. It covers the technical root cause, a practical step-by-step migration approach, and the PO library dependencies involved. 

What Is ASMA (Adapter-Specific Message Attributes)? 

When running integration scenarios in SAP Process Orchestration, integration developers often need to pass dynamic, context-specific values — such as a target filename, a dynamic URL path, or a SOAP action — from the sender adapter through the integration logic to a receiver adapter. The mechanism that enables this in SAP PO is called Adapter-Specific Message Attributes (ASMA). 

ASMA values are carried in a special container called Dynamic Configuration, which travels with the message throughout the SAP PO pipeline. They can be read and written in three ways: 

  1. UDF in Message Mapping: In User-Defined Functions (UDFs) within a message mapping using the Java API: DynamicConfiguration, DynamicConfigurationKey, StreamTransformationConstants.DYNAMIC_CONFIGURATION. 
  2. Java Mapping: In a standalone Java Mapping class that extends AbstractTransformation, using input.getDynamicConfiguration() to read or write ASMA values directly on the TransformationInput object. 
  3. XSLT Mapping: Via the SAP XI Mapping API extension functions provided by the SAP XML Toolkit. These extension functions allow an XSLT stylesheet to call into the Dynamic Configuration container to read and write ASMA values at runtime. 

Core API Dependencies for Dynamic Configuration 

Dynamic Configuration API depends on two specific JAR files that are part of the Process Orchestration runtime.  

JAR File 

Key Classes Provided 

Purpose 

com.sap.xpi.ib.mapping.lib.jar 

AbstractTransformation, TransformationInput, TransformationOutput, StreamTransformationException, AbstractTrace 

Core mapping framework. All Java Mappings must extend AbstractTransformation and implement the transform() method. 

com.sap.xi.mapping.tool.lib_api.jar 

DynamicConfiguration, DynamicConfigurationKey, StreamTransformationConstants 

Dynamic Configuration API. Provides the typed container for reading and writing Adapter-Specific Message Attributes by namespace/attribute key. 

 

So, how to migrate an Interface with ASMA Using the SAP Integration Suite capability? 

1. Create a Data Extraction in Migration Assessment (https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/migration-assessment) 

Run Migration Assessment first to connect to your SAP PO system, extract all integration artifacts, and evaluate each scenario. As we will explore in this post, extracting this data reveals both the complexity and the use of Dynamic Configuration. 

Rules identified: 

  • GMMCustomUDFUsageCount 
  • GMMCustomUDFDynamicConfiguration 
  • JAVAMDynamicConfiguration 

 2. Migration Tooling (https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/migration-tooling?q=migration+tool) 

Using the assessment results as input, proceed with the Migration Tool for scenarios classified as "Ready to migrate" or "Adjustment required." The tool semi-automatically generates iFlows in SAP Integration Suite based on templates derived from the SAP PO configuration.  

3.Outcome & Validation 

After the migration, validate the migrated iFlows. The combined use of both tools ensures a structured, risk-reduced, and cost-efficient transition from SAP PO/PI to SAP Integration Suite. 

What Happens When You Migrate the Interfaces with ASMA 

Let's have a look at this demo scenario: 

 

1. Request Payload 

2. Processing Logic 

  1. Java mapping reads the input message, gets "action" and puts it into Dynamic Configuration attribute "THeaderSOAPACTION". 
  2. XSLT Mapping converts the input into message into target format and store tag "message" into Dynamic Configuration attribute "inputMessage". 
  3. Receiver Communication Channel sets the Dynamic Configuration attribute "THeaderSOAPACTION" into SOAPAction. 
  4. Once the target system processes the message, there is a Graphical Mapping with “User Defined Functions (UDF)”. This function assign values coming from Dynamic Configuration Attributes, "THeaderSOAPACTION" and "inputMessage", and puts into the tags "dynAction" and "inputMessage" respectivelly. 

 3. Response Payload 

 

Once the interface is migrated, you will find in your Integration Suite package the artifacts generated based on your SAP PO objects. 

Check the Interface (flow). You will see boxes representing the logic that was defined on your SAP PO with wrapper functions depending on your ASMA use case.  

 

JAVA MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) – A wrapper class is generated by the Migration tool by default to provide temporary compatibility. While you can override the Java method to retain Dynamic Configuration logic, this approach is not recommended for production use. The preferred approach is to replace the Dynamic Configuration calls with message headers as described in the adaptation section below. 

 XSLT MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) - The migrated XSLT artifact retains the SAP XI Mapping API calls. These must be adapted to use Cloud Integration-native header access mechanisms. 

GRAPHICAL MAPPING (Using Dynamic Configuration) - UDFs referencing the Dynamic Configuration API are migrated as-is and will require manual adaptation. 

 

 

How to Adapt ASMA-Based Interfaces for Cloud Integration 

Evaluate your scenario — Identify all ASMA usages across Java Mappings, UDFs, and XSLT stylesheets. The Migration tool does not replace Dynamic Configuration logic. 

Understand how messages are processed by Cloud Integration. "Dynamic Values” are passed via named message headers or properties (set by a Content Modifier or Groovy Script step) not via a Dynamic Configuration container. 

 

 In SAP Cloud Integration, the correct mechanism for passing dynamic values through an integration flow is via message headers or exchange properties. These are set in dedicated flow steps before the mapping executes and then referenced in the mapping logic or adapter configuration. 

 

Concept 

SAP PO (Examples) 

SAP Cloud Integration (Examples) 

Dynamic value container 

  • Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) 
  • Message Headers 
  • Exchange Properties 

How values are set 

  • Java DynamicConfiguration.put() 
  • Content Modifier 
  • Groovy Script setProperty() / setHeaders() 

How values are read in mapping 

  • Java DynamicConfiguration.get() 
  • Content Modifier 
  • Graphical Mapping out-of-the-box functions to getProperty and getHeader 
  • Groovy Script getProperty() / getHeaders() 

Dynamic adapter config 

  • Adapter reads ASMA by namespace + attribute key 
  • Adapter uses expression ${header.HEADER_NAME} or ${property.PROPERTY_NAME} 

 

Therefore, if an imported mapping uses Dynamic Configuration APIs for accessing Adapter-Specific Message Attributes, the integration flow must be manually adjusted to ensure correct execution.  

 

References 

Links 

Creating Message Mapping as an Artifact 

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/developing-message-mapping-as-artifact?locale=en-US 

Headers and Exchange Properties Provided by the Integration Framework 

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/headers-and-exchange-properties-provided-by-integration-framework 

Access Headers and Properties in Scripts 

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/access-headers-and-properties-in-scripts 

 

 

Let's Review the Integration Flow Imported in the Previous Steps and Look into Adaptations 

 

 1. Java Mapping Replacement: A Content Modifier step is added before the mapping to set the SOAPAction message header and store the inputMessage value into message property. This replaces the Dynamic Configuration to write operations previously performed in Java Mapping. 

 

 2. XSLT Mapping: Remove Dynamic Configuration functionalities 

 

 3. Graphical Mapping: UDFs adapted to use out-of-the-box functions instead of the Dynamic Configuration API. 

 

 

 

 Additionally, for XI Adapter, Natively Supports ASMA 

The XI adapter in Cloud Integration natively supports Adapter-Specific Message Attributes. The XI sender adapter automatically creates a pair of exchange properties for each ASMA: one holding the value and one holding the namespace. When configuring ASMAs for the XI receiver adapter, both properties must be explicitly created. 

For scenarios using the XI adapter, the migration of ASMA-based logic can therefore be automated. For other adapter types, a similar approach applies, with the key distinction that the namespace property is typically not required. It is specifically needed by the XI receiver adapter, but not by other adapters. 

In pipeline-based integration approaches this is handled automatically within the generic XI inbound and XI outbound flows. Since exchange properties are not passed between flows, corresponding headers are created at the XI inbound step and then converted back to properties at the XI outbound step. 

For further details, see: 

References 

Links 

Generic Inbound and Outbound 

Generic Inbound and Outbound | SAP Help Portal 

 

Summary 

Migrating integration scenarios that use Dynamic Configuration (ASMA) from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite requires a multi-step approach. 

The recommended migration path combines two capabilities of SAP Integration Suite: Migration Assessment, to identify all affected integration scenarios and quantify ASMA usage through rules such as GMMCustomUDFDynamicConfiguratiom, GMMCustomUDFUsageCount and JAVAMDynamicConfiguration; and the Migration Tool, to semi-automatically generate iFlows from existing SAP PO artifacts. 

However, automated migration alone may not be sufficient. Depending on how Dynamic Configuration was used, each mapping type may requires targeted manual adaptation. 

By combining the structured assessment and tooling capabilities with these targeted adaptations, teams can achieve a controlled, risk-reduced migration from SAP PO/PI to SAP Integration Suite while preserving the intended runtime behavior of each interface.

entry.title: Integration Suite: Building an MCP Server for a SAP CI Monitoring Assistant
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Integration Suite, Message Processing Logs (MPL) are the primary tool for monitoring and troubleshooting interfaces. Whenever something fails, the same routine follows: log into the tenant, open Monitor, filter by artifact, drill into the failed message, read the error. In my previous blogs I looked at ways to make this easier — automating email alerts for failed interfaces, logging custom header properties for better MPL search, and storing logs in Database for long-term retention.

Now we are taking it a step further. SAP recently released the MCP Server capability in Integration Suite, which lets you expose any API as a set of tools that an AI agent can discover and call at runtime. So instead of clicking through the Monitor, you can simply ask an assistant in natural language — "show me all failed messages in the last 2 hours" or "why did the XYZ interface fail?" — and let it query the tenant for you.

In this blog we will build a CI Monitoring Assistant: a generic, reusable MCP Server that sits on top of the SAP Cloud Integration OData API and can be consumed from any MCP-compatible client such as VS Code or Claude Desktop.

What is MCP?

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI agents discover and invoke tools at runtime. You publish an MCP Server — a runtime endpoint that advertises a list of named, described tools — and the agent reads those descriptions to decide what to call and how. No hardcoding of API calls into the agent.

The problem we are solving

Standard MPL monitoring works, but it is entirely manual and click-driven. For operations teams this means:

  • Every check requires logging into the tenant and navigating the Monitor.
  • Correlating a failure across artifacts, error text and custom headers takes several drill-downs.
  • There is no easy way to ask a plain-language question and get a consolidated answer.

The SAP API Hub provides the OpenAPI JSON API specification for the SAP Cloud Integration OData API (MessageProcessingLogs). We retrieve that specification from SAP API Hub and convert it into OpenAPI 3.0 (converted using AI) so it can be used consistently by downstream tools and agents. The MCP Server then exposes the resulting read operations as tools, making monitoring conversational instead of manual.

Prerequisites

Before we start, make sure the following are in place:

  • Cloud Integration and API Management capabilities activated in Integration Suite.
  • The Integration Cell runtime enabled — go to Settings → Runtimes and confirm it shows Active. The MCP Server artifact runs on Integration Cell; it cannot be deployed to the classic Cloud Integration runtime.
    • MCP is available only in premium and enhanced edition plans, and in trial accounts.
  • The PI_Integration_Developer role collection assigned to your user in the BTP Cockpit.

If any capability or runtime is missing, activate it first — the MCP Server option will not appear until Integration Cell is running.

Solution overview

At a high level, the flow looks like this:

VS Code / Claude Desktop  (MCP client)
        |  MCP protocol
        v
   MCP Server artifact  (Integration Cell)
        |  HTTPS + CI OAuth
        v
   CI OData API  (MessageProcessingLogs)
        |
        v
   Your SAP Cloud Integration tenant

The MCP Server is created from an HTTP Endpoint with an OpenAPI 3.0 specification. We import the OpenAPI JSON that describes the monitoring operations, choose which operations become tools, add prompts and resources, configure the HTTPS adapter to authenticate to CI using OAuth, and deploy. The client then connects using a runtime service key.

Step-by-step guide

Step 1 – Enable the required capabilities

Activate Cloud Integration, API Management, and the Integration Cell runtime. Confirm under Settings → Runtimes that Integration Cell shows Active. This is the single most common blocker — the MCP Server artifact type will not be selectable otherwise.

Step 2 – Prepare the OpenAPI 3.0 JSON

The MCP Server needs an OpenAPI 3.0 specification. Each operationId in the spec becomes a tool name the agent can discover, and each description is what the agent reads to decide when and how to use the tool — so write the descriptions for the agent.

download - mcp-ci-message-processing-logs-openapi-3.0.json

Step 3 – Create the MCP Server artifact

Go to an existing or new Integration Package and create a new artifact of type: MCP Server. When prompted for the source type, choose HTTP Endpoint with OpenAPI Specification and upload the OpenAPI 3.0 JSON file you downloaded in Step 2. The wizard imports the spec and generates a list of tools from your operations.

Step 4 – Select tools, resources and prompts

  • Tools – select the operations you want to expose. Review the generated tool name and description for each; refine descriptions so the agent understands intent. (Max 15 tools can be selected per MCP Artifact)

  • Resources – add any static reference content the agent may need (for example, a list of interface names or a status-code cheat sheet). This is optional but improves answer quality.

  • Prompts – provide reusable prompt templates, e.g. "Summarise all failures in the last 2 hours grouped by interface." These give end users a guided starting point.

Step 5 – Create two runtime service keys (CI OData API + MCP Client Auth)

This solution needs two separate OAuth credentials, because two different calls happen and they use different Process Integration Runtime service plans:

  • CI OData Monitoring API (plan api) → used inside the MCP Server by the HTTPS receiver adapter to read Message Processing Logs.
  • MCP Client Authentication (plan integration-flow) → used by the AI client (VS Code / Claude Desktop) to authenticate to the deployed MCP Server endpoint.

Both are created from the Process Integration Runtime service in your BTP subaccount.

5a. Service key for the CI OData Monitoring API (plan: api)

This is the credential the HTTPS adapter uses in Step 6 to read monitoring data.

  1. In your BTP subaccount, go to Instances and Subscriptions → Create.
  2. Select service Process Integration Runtime, plan api and give the instance a name (e.g. it-rt-api-odata).
  3. On the Parameters step, add the monitoring-read role — MonitorDataRead (this grants read access to Message Processing Logs and related monitoring entities). Keep the grant type as Client Credentials and create it.
  4. On that instance, create a service key (e.g. it-rt-api-key-odata).
  5. From the service key, copy clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl (and the API url/host). These go into the Security Material referenced by the HTTPS adapter in Step 6.

Use only MonitorDataRead here — the assistant is read-only, so there is no reason to grant write or admin roles to the credential that reaches your monitoring data.

5b. Service key for MCP client authentication (plan: integration-flow)

This is the credential the AI client uses in Step 8 to call the deployed MCP Server endpoint on Integration Cell.

  1. In your BTP subaccount, go to Instances and Subscriptions → Create.
  2. Select service Process Integration Runtime, plan integration-flow and give the instance a name (e.g. it-rt-iflow-mcp).
  3. On the Parameters step, add the role API.invoke (required to invoke the deployed MCP endpoint). Keep the grant type as Client Credentials and create it.
  4. On that instance, create a service key (e.g. it-rt-iflow-key-mcp).
  5. From the service key, copy clientid, clientsecret and tokenurl . These are the credentials you enter in the MCP client (VS Code / Claude Desktop) to obtain an access token and call the MCP Server.

Step 6 – Configure the HTTPS adapter with CI OAuth

This is the authentication link between the MCP Server and the CI OData Monitoring API. Here you deploy the OAuth credential you created in Step 5a as a Security Material, then reference it in the HTTP receiver adapter of the MCP Server.

1. Deploy the Step 5a credentials as a Security Material - In Integration Suite, go to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Manage Security → Security Material → Create → OAuth2 Client Credentials. Fill in the values from your Step 5a service key. Save and deploy it. This keeps the CI credentials inside Integration Suite, away from the agent.

2. Update the HTTP receiver adapter in the MCP Server - Open the MCP Server artifact and edit the HTTP receiver connection that points to the CI OData Monitoring API:

  • Set Authentication to OAuth2 Client Credentials.
  • In Credential Name, select the Security Material you just deployed.

Save the changes. The MCP Server will now use the Step 5a OAuth credential to authenticate every call it forwards to the CI Monitoring API.

Step 7 – Deploy the MCP Server

Once tools, resources, prompts and authentication are configured, deploy the artifact. Deployment provisions the runtime endpoint on Integration Cell and makes the tools available for discovery. Wait for the status to show as deployed/active. Note down the MCP Server endpoint URL — the MCP path must be unique.

Step 8 – Test with MCP Client (VS Code / Claude Desktop)

Now connect an MCP client. You need two things:

  • MCP Server URL – the endpoint of your deployed MCP Server. Get it from Monitor → Manage Integration Content on the Integration Cell runtime: open the deployed MCP Server artifact and copy its endpoint URL.
  • Authentication – the OAuth credentials from your Step 5b service key: clientid, clientsecret and tokenurl.

Add these to your MCP client configuration:

Claude Desktop – add the MCP Server to the configuration file with the endpoint URL and the Step 5b OAuth credentials (client ID, client secret, token URL). Restart Claude Desktop; the tools should appear as available

VS Code – add the MCP Server in your MCP settings (or your extension's config), pointing to the same endpoint URL and Step 5b credentials.

Once connected, the client fetches a token from the token URL using the client Id and secret, then uses it to call the MCP Server. The server discovers your tools, and you can start asking questions in natural language:

  • "List all failed messages in the last 2 hours."
  • "Show the error details for the last failure of the OrderToCash interface."
  • "Find messages where the PO number custom header equals 4500001234."

The agent picks the right tool, calls the MCP Server, which forwards the request to the CI Monitoring API using the OAuth credentials, and returns a consolidated answer — no clicking through the Monitor.

Future Enhancements

  • Extend to more OData APIs – The Cloud Integration OData API exposes far more than Message Processing Logs. Using the exact same steps in this blog, you can build multiple MCP Servers — each wrapping a different API — to create, deploy, undeploy, update, or delete artifacts, parameters, and security materials conversationally.
  • Govern write access carefully – This MCP server is deliberately read-only. The moment you expose write operations the agent can change your tenant, so grant each service key only the minimum role, keep it off production first, and add human confirmation before any destructive action.
  • No rate limiting or payload protection is added in this basic setup. For production, put the API Artifact / MCP Gateway governance layer in front (rate limiting, audit logging, IP blocking).
  • Move to the SAP MCP Hub – SAP is building a centralized, no-code MCP Hub (announced Q4 2025, planned GA in H1 2026) that removes the need for each team to build and maintain its own MCP Servers. It handles cross-cutting concerns like security, authorization, observability, and protocol versioning centrally, and adds no-code OData/OpenAPI-to-MCP conversion, modular servers that combine tools from multiple APIs, destination-based connectivity, AI-assisted tool optimization, built-in validation, and a test chat. Once available, migrating this assistant to the MCP Hub is the natural next step.
  • MCP on Integration Suite is a new capability and is evolving quickly; screens and options may differ slightly from what you see when you try it. Check the SAP Help Portal (Design APIs and MCP Servers) for the latest.

This blog provides a basic, reusable foundation. You can enhance the MCP Server based on your own requirements — more tools, tighter filters, governance policies, or write-back operations behind proper approval.

Reference

  • One Blog, Two Strategic Topics: API-Centric Integration and MCP Gateway

Conclusion

The MCP Server capability turns SAP Integration Suite monitoring from a manual, click-driven task into a conversational one. By exposing the CI OData Monitoring API as a small set of well-described tools, any MCP-compatible client — VS Code, Claude Desktop, or others — becomes a CI Monitoring Assistant that operations teams can query in plain language.

If you see scope for enhancement in the tool set or the OpenAPI spec, feel free to drop a comment. I would love to hear how you extend it.

Happy Integrating!

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite : GitHub integration in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction 

SAP Cloud Integration version 6.80/(8.x --> 8.44 ), one of the capabilities of SAP Integration Suite, comes with a feature which will allow you to integrate the GitHub to the integration suite to perform the basic GitHub operations on the design time integration artifacts. To know more, you can refer the help documentation.

In this blog post, you will understand the detailed aspects of this feature, Why, What, its objectives, principles and how to leverage in the Cloud integration capability of the integration suite.

Why GitHub integration is essential?

  • No GitHub integration in the Integration Suite for collaborative development, track changes, storage, backup, or recovery.
  • Users must manually download and upload artifacts in GitHub, causing media breakage.
  • Manual process is slow, error-prone, and impacts version control, reducing productivity for effective collaborative developments.

What is GitHub Integration?

  • Integrates Integration Suite with GitHub for secure repository connection, Git authentication, and operations directly in-app.
  • Streamlines artifact management, reduces errors, enhances version control, and supports collaborative developments and CI/CD compatibility.

Benefits/Value proposition

  • Collaboration: Streamlined teamwork via branches and pull requests
  • Seamless Integration : No media breakage between Integration suite and GitHub
  • Governance : Github repositories maintained in Integration suite via Tenant administrator
  • DevOps: Enables CI/CD automation
  • Backup: Secure storage for artifacts with easy recovery.
  • Distributed: Work locally and sync changes.
  • Rollback: Revert to previous versions quickly.
  • Traceability: Track changes for auditing.
  • Scalability: Supports large and distributed teams.
  • Security: Protect artifacts with permissions and authentication.

Availability

GitHub integration in the integration suite for cloud integration capability is available in the trial, standard and above/higher editions.

How to leverage?

Let me explain the workflow and steps into two parts.

  • How to Successfully Configure GitHub Repositories for Cloud Integration?
  • How to successfully import/clone, push, Pull the integration artifacts from/to Github?

How to Successfully Configure GitHub Repositories for Cloud Integration?

To integrate GitHub repositories successfully, follow these easy steps:

Prerequisites:

  1. Ensure your account has a Tenant Admin role (refer to Cloud Integration documentation if unsure).
  2. Generate a Personal Access Token (PAT) with the required permissions for the repositories.

How to Create a Personal Access Token (PAT):

  1. Log in to GitHub, go to Settings -- >  Developer Settings -- > Personal Access Tokens.
  2. Choose Fine-Grained or Classic Tokens and click Generate New Token. Set an expiry for the token.

Simple Setup Steps:

  1. In the Cloud Integration portal, navigate to Settings -- > Integrations -- > Git Access.
  2. Click Edit in the Repository Details table and select Add to set up a new repository.
  3. Enter your PAT and GitHub repository URL.
  4. Click Connect to link the repository (Name and Description will auto-fill).
  5. Click Add to save the repository details in the table.

Notes:

  • You can integrate up to 50 repositories.
  • Make sure to click Save after completing all configurations.

Follow these steps to configure your GitHub repositories for seamless Cloud Integration!

View Github Repostories

Add Github Repository

Edit Github Repository 

How to successfully import/clone, push, Pull the integration artifacts from/to Github?

  • Git Import use case & experience 
  • Git Push use case & experience
  • Git Pull use case and experience

Git Import use case & experience 

Prerequisites:

  • Ensure Git repositories are configured by the tenant admin.
  • Have a Personal Access Token (PAT) with permission for the repositories.

Steps for Importing Integration Flows:

  1. In the Design section, go to Integration Packages.
  2. Select an integration package and click Edit.
  3. Open the Artifacts tab and select Add Integration Flow.
  4. Choose Add Integrations Manually (requires Premium plan).
  5. Use Git Import to import the flow:
    • Enter your PAT.
    • Select a repository and branch.
    • Choose the desired flow and click Add or Add and Open in Editor.

Outcome:
The flow is imported into the integration package, enabling Git operations like push and pull for collaboration and version control

Add Integration flow from Github repository, branch

Git Push use case & experience

Git Push lets you upload local changes from an integration artifact to a connected remote Git repository, ensuring your repository is fully updated for collaboration and version control.

Prerequisites Before Git Push:

  • Configured Repository: Your admin should set up the necessary repositories (see Git Access guide for details).

  • Access Permissions: Have the right permissions and a Personal Access Token (PAT) ready.

Steps to Push Changes:

  1. Navigate to Integration Packages: Go to Design > Integrations and APIs to find the list of integration packages.

  2. Select the Package: Choose the package with the artifact you want to push and click Artifacts.

  3. Start Git Push: Click the ... (triple dot) icon on the artifact and select Git Push.

  4. Fill in the Details:

    • Add your PAT for authentication.

    • Choose the repository name and branch.

  5. Commit Message: Enter a clear message describing your changes.

  6. Push Changes: Click Push to upload updates to the remote repository.

Key Notes:

  • The repository will be updated with the latest version of your artifact.

  • Ensure branch and repository details are correct to avoid errors or conflicts.

Git Push operation to push the local changes to the Github repository, branch

Git Pull use case and experience

Git Pull updates your local files by fetching and merging the latest changes from a remote Git repository, keeping everything in sync.

Before You Start

  1. Ensure repositories are set up by the administrator (Check Git Access documentation).
  2. Git Pull works only for imported artifacts.
Steps to Perform Git Pull
  1. Open Integration Packages:
    Go to Design -- > Integrations and APIs to find integration packages.
  2. Select a Package:
    Click on the package and then choose Artifacts to view its contents.
  3. Use Git Pull:
    Find the artifact to update and click the Git Pull icon.
  4. Verify Details:
    Confirm the displayed artifact info and enter your Personal Access Token (PAT).
  5. Finish Up:
    Click Pull to update your local artifact with the latest changes.

This helps you easily keep your files updated for smooth collaboration.

Git Pull operation to pull the latest changes from the Github repository, branch

FAQs

  1. Supported Integration Artifacts: Basic Git operations are supported for Integration Flows in the initial release.
  2. Available Editions: GitHub integration is available in the Trial, Standard, and higher editions of the Integration Suite.
  3. Repository Limit: You can add up to 50 GitHub repositories in the integration settings.
  4. Impact of Removing Repositories: Removing repositories from the integration settings has no effect on integration flows—they remain in the design workspace and GitHub, if previously pushed. However, the integration flow will be de-linked from GitHub.
  5. Supported Platforms: Only GitHub on the cloud is supported, on-premise GitHub is not supported.
  6. Cloud Platform to be supported in future: GitLab and BitBucket, 
  7. Authentication: GitHub operations are supported through PAT tokens only.
  8. PAT Token Persistence: PAT tokens are not stored, you must manually provide one for each GitHub operation.
  9. Repository Descriptions: Descriptions for GitHub repositories can be set during creation on GitHub and will be visible when imported into the Integration Suite.
  10. Differentiate local and remote changes: Github repository name, branch name is tagged to the integration flow available in the remote branch. Integration flow is tagged in the design workspace and monitoring view(for deployed artifact).

What’s coming next?

  • Introduce CRUD functionality for user Personal Access Tokens (PAT).
  • Add support for more authentication types and cloud platforms like GitLab.
  • Extend compatibility with on-premise GitHub repositories.
  • Enhance Git operations like import, push, pull for the remaining integration artifacts.
  • Implement conflict resolution tools (graphical/text editor) for integration artifacts and resources.
  • Support multi-branch management for integration artifacts.
  • Enable public OData Remote APIs for CI/CD processes.
  • Expand Git support for integration packages.

Other source of material

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/git-access
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/git-push
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/git-pull
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/import-integration-flows-from-git-repository

I hope you find the feature in this blog post beneficial. If you have any questions or feedback, please leave a comment.

entry.title: Useful functions for XSLT in SAP CPI
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite 

Following up on my previous blog post Useful functions for XSLT 2.0 in SAP PO, where we leveraged Java extension calls and standard XSLT features within the Process Orchestration engine, this article addresses a common gap encountered when transitioning to or developing natively in SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration / CPI).

In SAP Integration Suite, it is is well-documented for Groovy how to manipulate the Message Processing Log with the following methods

  • addCustomHeaderProperty
  • setStringProperty or
  • addAttachmentAsString

(see Add Information to the Message Processing Log).

However, when executing XSLT 2.0/3.0 transformations, no native XSLT-level mechanism exists to directly interact with the MPL context. The lightweight Java utility below serves as a bridge to fill this gap.

Overview & Design Principles

The MPLWriter class uses OSGi runtime reflection and service lookup to interact directly with the CPI execution context passed into Java extension calls in Saxon/XSLT steps.

  • Compile-Time Dependencies:
    Only one external jar is needed: org.osgi.core-6.0.0.jar.
    (No SAP CPI runtime JARs are needed in your build environment.)

Legal Disclaimer & License

MIT License

Copyright (c) HO2 Systemberatung GmbH

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy
of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal
in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights
to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell
copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is
furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:

The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
copies or substantial portions of the Software.

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.

Disclaimer: This code is provided as-is without any warranty for correctness, completeness, or fitness for a particular purpose. It relies on non-public runtime structures resolved via reflection. This solution is completely independent and/or completely unsupported by SAP, by myself and/or my company - use it at your own risk 🙂

Source Code: MPLWriter.java

/*
 * Copyright (c) HO2 Systemberatung GmbH
 * Licensed under the MIT License.
 */
package script;

import java.io.ByteArrayInputStream;
import java.lang.reflect.Method;
import java.net.URI;
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.Map;
import java.util.Set;

import org.osgi.framework.Bundle;
import org.osgi.framework.BundleContext;
import org.osgi.framework.FrameworkUtil;
import org.osgi.framework.ServiceReference;

/**
 * Utility class to interact with SAP Integration Suite Message Processing Log (MPL)
 * dynamically from XSLT Java Extensions via OSGi and Reflection.
 * 
 * @Author HO2 Systemberatung GmbH
 */
public class MPLWriter {

	private static int debugCounter = 0;

	/**
	 * Sets a string property on the Message Processing Log.
	 */
	public static Object setStringProperty(Object mpl, String name, Object value) throws Exception {

		Object key = createTypedKey(mpl, name, String.class);
		return put(mpl, key, value);
	}

	/**
	 * Adds a custom header property (User Defined Attribute) to the MPL.
	 */
	public static String addCustomHeaderProperty(Object mpl, String name, String value) throws Exception {

		return addCustomHeaderProperty(mpl, name, value, false);
	}

	public static String addCustomHeaderProperty(Object mpl, String name, String value, boolean debugEnabled)
			throws Exception {

		debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "START addCustomHeaderProperty " + name + "=" + value);

		Object rootMpl = mpl.getClass().getMethod("getRoot").invoke(mpl);
		ClassLoader loader = mpl.getClass().getClassLoader();
		Class<?> udaClass = loader.loadClass("com.sap.it.op.mpl.UserDefinedAttributeTypeV2");
		Class<?> keysClass = loader.loadClass("com.sap.it.op.mpl.TypedMessageProcessingLogKeys");
		Object udaKey = keysClass.getField("TK_USER_DEFINED_ATTRIBUTES").get(null);
		Method get = rootMpl.getClass().getMethod("get", udaKey.getClass());
		Object uda = get.invoke(rootMpl, udaKey);

		if (uda == null) {

			uda = udaClass.getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance();

			rootMpl.getClass().getMethod("put", udaKey.getClass(), Object.class).invoke(rootMpl, udaKey, uda);
		}

		Method grantAttribute = udaClass.getMethod("grantAttribute", String.class);

		@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
		Set<String> values = (Set<String>) grantAttribute.invoke(uda, name);

		values.add(value);
		debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "UDA final=" + uda);

		return "OK";
	}

	/**
	 * Adds an attachment to the Message Processing Log (Normal Mode).
	 */
	public static String addAttachmentAsString(Object mpl, String name, String content, String mimeType)
			throws Exception {

		return addAttachmentAsString(mpl, name, content, mimeType, false);
	}

	/**
	 * Adds an attachment to the Message Processing Log (Debug Mode).
	 */
	public static String addAttachmentAsString(Object mpl, String name, String content, String mimeType,
			boolean debugEnabled) throws Exception {
		
		debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "START addAttachment name=" + name);

		// Obtain OSGi bundle context dynamically via Message class loader
		Bundle bundle = FrameworkUtil.getBundle(Class.forName("com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message"));
		debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "Bundle=" + bundle);

		BundleContext ctx = bundle.getBundleContext();
		ServiceReference<?> ref = ctx.getServiceReference("com.sap.esb.camel.message.storage.api.MessageStorageWrite");

		debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "MessageStorage reference=" + ref);

		if (ref == null) {

			return "MessageStorageWrite service not found";
		}

		Object messageStore = null;

		try {
			messageStore = ctx.getService(ref);
			Class<?> messageClass = messageStore.getClass().getClassLoader()
					.loadClass("com.sap.esb.camel.message.storage.api.Message");
			Object attachmentMessage = messageClass.getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance();

			/*
			 * Set Attachment Headers
			 */
			Map<String, String> headers = new HashMap<>();
			headers.put("SapAttachmentName", name);
			headers.put("SapAttachmentContentType", mimeType);

			messageClass.getMethod("setHeader", Map.class).invoke(attachmentMessage, headers);

			/*
			 * Set Body Content
			 */
			byte[] bytes = content.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);

			debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "Attachment bytes=" + bytes.length);

			Method setBody = null;

			for (Method m : messageClass.getMethods()) {
				if ("setBody".equals(m.getName()) && m.getParameterCount() == 1) {
					setBody = m;
					break;
				}
			}

			if (setBody == null) {
				throw new Exception("Message.setBody not found");
			}

			debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "setBody type=" + setBody.getParameterTypes()[0].getName());
			setBody.invoke(attachmentMessage, new ByteArrayInputStream(bytes));

			/*
			 * Resolve MPL Identifiers
			 */
			Object messageGuid = getMplValue(mpl, "MessageGuid");
			Object stepId = getMplValue(mpl, "StepId");

			debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "MessageGuid=" + messageGuid);
			debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "StepId=" + stepId);

			if (messageGuid == null) {
				throw new Exception("MessageGuid not found");
			}

			messageClass.getMethod("setMplID", String.class).invoke(attachmentMessage, messageGuid.toString());

			if (stepId != null) {
				messageClass.getMethod("setStepID", String.class).invoke(attachmentMessage, stepId.toString());
			}

			/*
			 * Tag Message as ATTACHMENT
			 */
			Class<?> tagClass = messageClass.getClassLoader()
					.loadClass("com.sap.esb.camel.message.storage.api.Message$TagKey");
			@SuppressWarnings({ "unchecked", "rawtypes" })
			Object attachmentTag = Enum.valueOf((Class) tagClass, "ATTACHMENT");

			messageClass.getMethod("setTagKey", tagClass).invoke(attachmentMessage, attachmentTag);

			/*
			 * Persist Attachment to Storage
			 */
			URI uri = (URI) messageStore.getClass().getMethod("add", messageClass, boolean.class).invoke(messageStore,
					attachmentMessage, true);
			debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "Attachment URI=" + uri);

			/*
			 * Register Attachment URI in MPL Attachment List
			 */
			Object attachments = getMplValue(mpl, "attachments");
			debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "Existing attachments=" + attachments);

			if (attachments == null) {
				attachments = new ArrayList<>();
				Object key = createTypedKey(mpl, "attachments", List.class);
				put(mpl, key, attachments);
			}

			Class<?> attachmentType = mpl.getClass().getClassLoader().loadClass("com.sap.it.op.mpl.AttachmentType");
			Object attachment = attachmentType.getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance();

			attachmentType.getMethod("setName", String.class).invoke(attachment, name);
			attachmentType.getMethod("setURI", URI.class).invoke(attachment, uri);

			@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
			List<Object> list = (List<Object>) attachments;

			list.add(attachment);
			debug(mpl, debugEnabled, "Attachment list size=" + list.size());

			return "CREATED URI=" + uri;

		} finally {
			if (ref != null) {
				ctx.ungetService(ref);
			}
		}
	}

	private static Object createTypedKey(Object mpl, String name, Class<?> type) throws Exception {
		Class<?> keyClass = findKeyClass(mpl);
		Method grant = keyClass.getMethod("grantKey", Class.class, String.class);

		return grant.invoke(null, type, name);
	}

	private static Class<?> findKeyClass(Object mpl) throws Exception {

		for (Method m : mpl.getClass().getMethods()) {

			if ("put".equals(m.getName()) && m.getParameterCount() == 2) {
				Class<?>[] params = m.getParameterTypes();
				if (params[1].equals(Object.class)) {
					return params[0];
				}
			}
		}

		throw new Exception("TypedMessageProcessingLogKey not found");
	}

	private static Object put(Object mpl, Object key, Object value) throws Exception {

		Method put = mpl.getClass().getMethod("put", key.getClass(), Object.class);

		return put.invoke(mpl, key, value);
	}

	private static Object getMplValue(Object mpl, String name) throws Exception {

		Class<?> keyClass = findKeyClass(mpl);
		Object key = createTypedKey(mpl, name, guessKeyType(name));
		Method get = mpl.getClass().getMethod("get", keyClass);

		return get.invoke(mpl, key);
	}

	private static Class<?> guessKeyType(String name) {

		switch (name) {
		case "MessageGuid":
		case "StepId":
			return String.class;
		case "attachments":
			return List.class;
		default:
			return Object.class;
		}
	}

	private static synchronized void debug(Object mpl, boolean enabled, Object value) {

		if (!enabled) {
			return;
		}

		try {
			setStringProperty(mpl, "MPL_DEBUG_" + String.format("%02d", ++debugCounter), String.valueOf(value));
		} catch (Exception ignored) {

		}
	}
}

XSLT Call Example

Below there is a sample xsl stylesheet to make use of the little helper.

Prerequisite

There MUST be a groovy script step before in your iFlow, otherwise SAP_MessageProcessingLog is not available/injected.
It is not required, that the script has any logic, it can directly return the message.

ℹ️Output generated by saxon:trace is redirected to the standard application server logging channel. In CPI, you can find these log lines directly in the web UI under Monitoring > System-Logs (or by downloading the tail/system log file for the node execution).

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xsl:stylesheet version="3.0" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xmlns:saxon="http://saxon.sf.net/" xmlns:mpl="java:script.MPLWriter" exclude-result-prefixes="xs saxon mpl">
  <!-- SAP CPI injects the Message Processing Log object -->
  <xsl:param name="SAP_MessageProcessingLog"/>
  <!-- Enable MPL debug -->
  <xsl:param name="debugEnabled" select="true()"/>
  <!-- Saxon template execution tracing -->
  <xsl:mode on-multiple-match="use-last" use-accumulators="itemCount totalQuantity" saxon:trace="yes"/>
  <!-- Count items -->
  <xsl:accumulator name="itemCount" as="xs:integer" initial-value="0" saxon:trace="yes">
    <xsl:accumulator-rule match="Item" select="$value + 1"/>
  </xsl:accumulator>
  <!-- Sum quantities -->
  <xsl:accumulator name="totalQuantity" as="xs:integer" initial-value="0" saxon:trace="yes">
    <xsl:accumulator-rule match="Quantity/text()" select="$value + xs:integer(.)"/>
  </xsl:accumulator>
  <xsl:template match="/">
    <xsl:variable name="orderId" select="string(Order/Header/ID)"/>
    <xsl:variable name="customer" select="string(Order/Header/CustomerNumber)"/>
    <!-- MPL properties -->
    <xsl:variable name="status1" select="mpl:setStringProperty($SAP_MessageProcessingLog,'XSLT_STATUS','STARTED')"/>
    <xsl:variable name="orderProperty" select="mpl:setStringProperty($SAP_MessageProcessingLog,'ORDER_ID',$orderId)"/>
    <!-- MPL Custom Header Properties -->
    <xsl:variable name="header1" select="mpl:addCustomHeaderProperty($SAP_MessageProcessingLog,'PROCESSING_TYPE','XSLT3_DEMO',$debugEnabled)"/>
    <xsl:variable name="header2" select="mpl:addCustomHeaderProperty($SAP_MessageProcessingLog,'CUSTOMER_NUMBER',$customer,$debugEnabled)"/>
    <!-- HTML attachment -->
    <xsl:variable name="html" select="
      mpl:addAttachmentAsString(
        $SAP_MessageProcessingLog,
        'order-summary.html',
        concat(
          '<html>',
          '<body>',
          '<h1>SAP CPI XSLT 3.0 Demo</h1>',
          '<h2>Order ',
          $orderId,
          '</h2>',
          '<p>Customer: ',
          $customer,
          '</p>',
          '<table border="1">',
          '<tr><th>Material</th><th>Quantity</th></tr>',
          string-join(
            for $i in Order/Items/Item
            return concat(
              '<tr>',
              '<td>',
              $i/Material,
              '</td>',
              '<td>',
              $i/Quantity,
              '</td>',
              '</tr>'
            ),
            ''
          ),
          '</table>',
          '<p>Items: ',
          accumulator-after('itemCount'),
          '</p>',
          '<p>Total Quantity: ',
          accumulator-after('totalQuantity'),
          '</p>',
          '</body>',
          '</html>'
        ),
        'text/html',
        $debugEnabled
      )
    "/>
    <!-- Store calculated values -->
    <xsl:variable name="itemCount" select="mpl:setStringProperty($SAP_MessageProcessingLog,'ITEM_COUNT',string(accumulator-after('itemCount')))"/>
    <xsl:variable name="quantity" select="mpl:setStringProperty($SAP_MessageProcessingLog,'TOTAL_QUANTITY',string(accumulator-after('totalQuantity')))"/>
    <xsl:variable name="status2" select="mpl:setStringProperty($SAP_MessageProcessingLog,'XSLT_STATUS','COMPLETED')"/>
    <!-- XML result -->
    <OrderResult>
      <Header>
        <ID>
          <xsl:value-of select="$orderId"/>
        </ID>
        <Customer>
          <xsl:value-of select="$customer"/>
        </Customer>
      </Header>
      <Items>
        <xsl:apply-templates select="Order/Items/Item"/>
      </Items>
      <Summary>
        <ItemCount>
          <xsl:value-of select="accumulator-after('itemCount')"/>
        </ItemCount>
        <TotalQuantity>
          <xsl:value-of select="accumulator-after('totalQuantity')"/>
        </TotalQuantity>
      </Summary>
    </OrderResult>
  </xsl:template>
  <!-- Item transformation -->
  <xsl:template match="Item">
    <Item>
      <Material>
        <xsl:value-of select="Material"/>
      </Material>
      <Quantity>
        <xsl:value-of select="Quantity"/>
      </Quantity>
    </Item>
  </xsl:template>
</xsl:stylesheet>

Input XML Example

Below is a sample input XML payload processed by the XSLT mapping above, from which the Order ID is dynamically extracted and pushed into the MPL Custom Header Property:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Order>
  <Header>
    <ID>4500012345</ID>
    <Date>2026-07-17</Date>
    <CustomerNumber>1002934</CustomerNumber>
    <Priority>HIGH</Priority>
    <SalesOrganization>1710</SalesOrganization>
  </Header>
  <Items>
    <Item>
      <LineNumber>10</LineNumber>
      <Material>MAT-8842</Material>
      <Description>Industrial Sensor</Description>
      <Quantity>50</Quantity>
      <Unit>EA</Unit>
      <NetValue>1250.00</NetValue>
    </Item>
    <Item>
      <LineNumber>20</LineNumber>
      <Material>MAT-9921</Material>
      <Description>Control Module</Description>
      <Quantity>25</Quantity>
      <Unit>EA</Unit>
      <NetValue>3750.00</NetValue>
    </Item>
    <Item>
      <LineNumber>30</LineNumber>
      <Material>MAT-5500</Material>
      <Description>Mounting Bracket</Description>
      <Quantity>100</Quantity>
      <Unit>EA</Unit>
      <NetValue>500.00</NetValue>
    </Item>
    <Item>
      <LineNumber>40</LineNumber>
      <Material>MAT-7711</Material>
      <Description>Power Supply</Description>
      <Quantity>10</Quantity>
      <Unit>EA</Unit>
      <NetValue>900.00</NetValue>
    </Item>
  </Items>
</Order>

Result

  • Custom Header +
  • Attachment +
  • String Properties in the text log created

ℹ️It will also create the following output under https://<cpi-ui>/shell/monitoring/SystemLogs

2026-07-17 14:55:17#+0000#ERROR#com.sap.esb.camel.route.policy.SingletonExchangeSelectionPolicy#anonymous#1784300116738-Blog_Xslt_Functions_Worker-1#com.sap.esb.camel.quartz.camel.route.policy#na#na#na#na#[INFO] MPL: AGpaQlWi5X9iHy9FgbgPV5XNRkVS ; Acquired lock: a6fa451d-daa0-4d18-94af-81c9d8d6897c_RONCE_1492 to start route: route34#-#10.0.201.0#0
<trace saxon-version="9.9.1.6" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform">
 <source node="/" line="0" file="">
  <rule match="document-node()" line="27" module="XSLTMapping1.xsl">
totalQuantity BEFORE /Order/Items[1]/Item[1]/Quantity[1]/text()[1]: 50
totalQuantity BEFORE /Order/Items[1]/Item[3]/Quantity[1]/text()[1]: 175
itemCount BEFORE /Order/Items[1]/Item[1]: 1
itemCount BEFORE /Order/Items[1]/Item[4]: 4
itemCount BEFORE /Order/Items[1]/Item[2]: 2
totalQuantity BEFORE /Order/Items[1]/Item[2]/Quantity[1]/text()[1]: 75
itemCount BEFORE /Order/Items[1]/Item[3]: 3
totalQuantity BEFORE /Order/Items[1]/Item[4]/Quantity[1]/text()[1]: 185
   </source><!-- /Order/Items[1]/Item[2] -->
    </rule>
   </source><!-- /Order/Items[1]/Item[1] -->
   <source node="/Order/Items[1]/Item[1]" line="-1" file="">
    </rule>
    <rule match="element(Q{}Item)" line="136" module="XSLTMapping1.xsl">
    <rule match="element(Q{}Item)" line="136" module="XSLTMapping1.xsl">
   <source node="/Order/Items[1]/Item[2]" line="-1" file="">
   <source node="/Order/Items[1]/Item[3]" line="-1" file="">
    <rule match="element(Q{}Item)" line="136" module="XSLTMapping1.xsl">
   <source node="/Order/Items[1]/Item[4]" line="-1" file="">
    <rule match="element(Q{}Item)" line="136" module="XSLTMapping1.xsl">
  </rule>
</trace>
 </source><!-- / -->
   </source><!-- /Order/Items[1]/Item[4] -->
    </rule>
    </rule>
   </source><!-- /Order/Items[1]/Item[3] -->

If you found this article helpful, please leave a Like! You can also enjoy reading other community contributions and stay up to date by browsing SAP Cloud Integration Blog Posts.

entry.title: Building CPI Explorer – My Journey to Simplify Searching SAP Integration Suite iFlows
entry.content:
Building CPI Explorer – My Journey to Simplify Searching SAP Integration Suite iFlows

Introduction

As an SAP Integration Suite consultant, a large part of my day involves supporting existing integrations, troubleshooting production issues, and understanding how different systems are connected. One challenge kept coming up repeatedly: finding where a particular SFTP directory, HTTP endpoint, ProcessDirect endpoint, or credential was configured across dozens (sometimes hundreds) of Integration Flows.

SAP's Design-Time APIs let you retrieve Integration Packages and Integration Flows, but there's no built-in way to search adapter configurations across an entire tenant. In practice, that meant opening package after package and manually inspecting each iFlow — slow, repetitive, and easy to get wrong in a large landscape.

Instead of continuing to do this by hand, I decided to build a tool to solve it for myself, and hopefully for other developers too.

Where It All Started

I started my career as an ABAP developer, and one feature I relied on constantly was the "Where Used" list. If I needed to know where a table, function module, or field was being used, I could just look it up. It was simple, fast, and always available.

When I moved into PI/PO and later CPI, that convenience disappeared. There's no equivalent way to instantly search where something is used across your integration landscape.

This became a real problem in day-to-day support. An FICO or SD colleague would come to me and say "my file isn't moving, can you check?" Half the time, they weren't even sure whether their interface was flowing through PI/CPI at all — they just knew something was stuck and assumed I'd know where to look. Other times, someone from the infra team would ask "are we using this host anywhere? We need to update it" or "can you find every flow using this username, we're rotating credentials." Each of these meant manually opening iFlow after iFlow, hoping I didn't miss one.

That's where the idea for CPI Explorer actually came from — not as a big architectural vision, but as a way to get back something as basic as the ABAP "Where Used" list, just for CPI.

One real example was during an infrastructure change where we had to update an SFTP host. Before CPI Explorer, the only option was to manually open multiple Integration Flows and inspect every SFTP adapter. With CPI Explorer, I can simply search for the hostname and immediately see every Integration Flow that references it.

The Idea

The question I started with was simple:

 

                   Can I build a "Where Used" list for CPI?

I spent quite some time exploring the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and Design-Time APIs to see whether this functionality already existed. The Design-Time APIs gave me metadata, but not the adapter-level detail I needed. So I went a level deeper: download each Integration Flow artifact, extract the .iflw file, parse the XML, and build a local searchable index from it.

Once that was working, I wrapped it in a Chrome Extension so I could search directly from the browser instead of running scripts every time.

How CPI Explorer Works

At a high level, the tool:

  • Authenticates with SAP Integration Suite using OAuth
  • Retrieves Integration Packages and Integration Flows via the Design-Time APIs
  • Downloads Integration Flow artifacts
  • Extracts and parses the .iflw XML files
  • Builds a searchable metadata index
  • Lets you search adapter properties from the Chrome Extension

It currently supports searching for:

  • SFTP directories
  • Host names
  • HTTP endpoints
  • ProcessDirect endpoints
  • JMS queues
  • Credentials
  • Externalized parameters
  • Other adapter properties

What I Learned

Building this taught me more than I expected about:

  • SAP Integration Suite Design-Time APIs
  • The structure of Integration Flow artifacts
  • Parsing .iflw XML files
  • Chrome Extension development
  • Organizing a Node.js application into reusable modules

The trickiest part was making sure every downloaded artifact mapped back correctly to its source Integration Flow while the index was being built. Getting that mapping right was what made the search results trustworthy across a whole tenant.

Why I'm Sharing This

I built CPI Explorer to solve my own problem, but I believe many SAP Integration Suite developers face the same challenge. That's why I decided to make it open source, so others can use it, contribute ideas, and help improve it over time.

GitHub

The project is available here: https://github.com/lokeshbhukya2019-netizen/cpi-explorer

Feedback, suggestions, and contributions are welcome.

What's Next

A few things I'm planning to add:

  • Search by adapter type
  • Multi-tenant support
  • Export to Excel/CSV
  • Dependency visualization
  • Support for additional adapter types

If you have ideas for other useful features, I'd like to hear them.

Closing Thoughts

CPI Explorer started as an attempt to fix a problem I ran into constantly in day-to-day SAP Integration Suite work. Along the way it turned into a good excuse to learn more about SAP's APIs, artifact structures, and browser extension development.

I hope it saves other SAP Integration Suite developers some time during troubleshooting and impact analysis. If you try it, I'd genuinely like to hear how it goes.

Thank you for taking the time to read about CPI Explorer. If you try it out, I'd love to hear your feedback, suggestions, or ideas for new features. I hope it helps make troubleshooting and impact analysis in SAP Integration Suite a little easier for the community.

Happy integrating!

entry.title: Migrate from SAP Neo, Cloud Integration to SAP Integration Suite with Confidence
entry.content:
Migrating from SAP Cloud Integration in the Neo environment to SAP Integration Suite on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry can raise many questions. To help you navigate this transition, we've published a comprehensive guide that brings together SAP documentation, best practices, and SAP support services to support every stage of your migration journey.

What You'll Learn

This guide walks you through the migration following the SAP Activate methodology, covering every phase from planning to post-go-live optimization:

  • Discover – Understand why SAP BTP Cloud Foundry is the strategic direction and explore the benefits of SAP Integration Suite.
  • Prepare – Assess your existing integration landscape and learn how the free Neo Sunset Migration Factory can help you create a migration roadmap.
  • Explore – Discover onboarding resources, hands-on workshops, consulting services, and best practices to accelerate your migration.
  • Realize & Deploy – Learn about SAP migration tools, Postman collections, testing strategies, and cutover recommendations for a successful go-live.
  • Run – Optimize and monitor your integration landscape with SAP Cloud ALM, Continuous Quality Checks (CQC), Cloud Application Services (CAS), and Expert-Guided Implementations.

How This Guide Helps

Whether you're just starting your migration planning or already executing your project, this article brings together key information on:

  • Official SAP migration resources
  • Available SAP Enterprise Support services
  • Adoption and enablement offerings
  • SAP Consulting services
  • Best practices for reducing migration risk and accelerating time-to-value

Access the Guide

Explore this guide "How to Migrate from SAP Neo, Cloud Integration to SAP Integration Suite through Support Services" on the SAP Business Technology Platform Value Map. To access Value Map Groups, you need to login to the SAP Community using an S-user ID or a Universal ID.

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps  

Beyond this topic, SAP Enterprise Support value maps on SAP Community provides guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next—all included in your SAP cloud subscription.

👉 Watch short demo video, then head over to our page - explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.   

 

About the author:  

Carol Zhang is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). With more than 20 years of experience at SAP and a background spanning roles in SAP Service and Support organization, she is passionate about helping customers succeed businesses accelerate the adoption of SAP BTP tools and services through SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

entry.title: Export SAP Integration Suite Inventory to Excel: Two Python Scripts for CPI iFlows and API Proxies.
entry.content:
If you have ever been asked how many iFlows sit on a tenant, or which API proxies belong to which product, you already know how it goes. You open the UI, click into one package at a time, and copy names into a spreadsheet by hand. It takes a while, and the list is out of date the moment someone adds a new flow. I got tired of doing this, so I wrote a couple of small scripts to do it for me.

There are two scripts. Each one reads a different part of SAP Integration Suite and writes the result straight into an Excel file. They use the standard public OData APIs with an OAuth client credential, so nothing is installed on the tenant. You run them from your own machine and you get a clean spreadsheet at the end.

Here is the one thing to keep straight while you read. There are two separate scripts for two separate jobs:

  • cpi_inventory_export.py reads Cloud Integration and lists every integration package and the iFlows inside each one.
  • apim_inventory_export.py reads API Management and lists every API proxy and the product it belongs to.

They look alike and run the same way, but they connect to different services in BTP and need different roles. To avoid any mix up, I have written each one as its own part below. The download link for both is at the end.

What each script produces

Script Reads from Output file Sheets you get

cpi_inventory_export.pyCloud IntegrationCPI_Inventory.xlsxPackages, iFlows
apim_inventory_export.pyAPI ManagementAPIM_Inventory.xlsxAPI Proxies, Products
